insights: Product Management and Customer Support departments account for 49.44% of total spending, indicating a strong concentration of financial activity in just two departments and potential over-reliance on these units for operational execution.

Services and Assets categories represent 67.7% of total expenses, with Services alone contributing nearly 39%—highlighting a structural dependency on external service providers and capital-intensive asset procurement.

A critical data quality failure affects 38.9% of records: negative processing times (ranging from -7064.78 to -3.05 hours) are present across all departments and categories, confirming a systemic calculation or timestamping error that invalidates any efficiency-based workflow assessment.

The expense processing workflow exhibits a severe bottleneck, with 55.76% of records in active states (Submitted + Pending), and Pending records exceeding Processed by 10%—indicating growing backlog and insufficient throughput capacity.

IT department bears the highest workflow pressure at 64.10% active states—8.34 percentage points above the overall average—and accounts for the highest rate of negative processing times (52.56%), suggesting its processes are both operationally strained and most impacted by the underlying data corruption.

Travel category has the most acute workflow bottleneck at 62.31% active states, and—along with Services (58.43%)—accounts for 64.25% of all active work, revealing that high-cost, time-sensitive expense types are disproportionately stalled in the pipeline.

Department-category spending interactions are moderate rather than concentrated: no single combination exceeds 10% of total spending, and the largest (Product Management × Services) accounts for only 9.66%, indicating spending is distributed across functional and cost-type boundaries without strong coupling.
summary: The analysis reveals pronounced imbalances in departmental spending—with Product Management and Customer Support driving nearly half of all expenses—and sharp concentration in cost categories, where Services and Assets dominate 67.7% of outlays. Critically, the processing_time_hours field is compromised by a systemic data quality issue: 38.9% of records show negative values across all departments and categories, rendering efficiency metrics unusable and pointing to a foundational timestamp or logic error in the workflow system. Concurrently, the workflow itself faces a severe operational bottleneck, with over half of all expenses stuck in Submitted or Pending states—especially in the IT department and Travel category—signaling urgent capacity constraints and risk of delayed approvals or compliance exposure. Given the global nature of the data corruption and the severity of the workflow stall, the immediate priority must be root-cause investigation of the negative time generation mechanism, followed by targeted triage of the IT and Travel processing queues to restore throughput and mitigate financial control risk.
